scriptFull:
In the days after the breach became public, the FBI did something that captured the entire situation in a single institutional moment: its cybersecurity officials publicly urged Americans to stop using regular phone calls and text messages for anything sensitive, recommending instead that people switch to Signal, FaceTime, and end-to-end encrypted applications. The agency whose predecessor law — CALEA — had created the vulnerability was now telling the public to route around that vulnerability, because the system it had spent three decades insisting was secure had just been used by a foreign intelligence service to read its own wiretap logs.
The debate over encryption backdoors has been running since 1994. Law enforcement has consistently argued that end-to-end encryption — the kind that cannot be intercepted even by the carrier — makes it effectively impossible to investigate criminals and terrorists, and so they have pushed for keys, for backdoors, for lawful access mechanisms that preserve the government's ability to read what it needs to read. The security community has consistently responded that no such system can be built securely, that any mechanism designed to allow authorized access will inevitably be exploited by unauthorized access, and that a backdoor cannot distinguish between a federal judge issuing a lawful order and a foreign intelligence service looking for an open door.
Salt Typhoon is not a theoretical argument in that debate. It is the empirical proof. The system built for authorized access became the entry point for the most consequential telecommunications breach in American history, China read American wiretaps for years without detection, and the FBI is now urging people to use tools that make CALEA-style interception technically impossible. The argument is settled. The wrong side won it in 1994, and the country has spent the years since building the infrastructure that would eventually make that defeat catastrophic.
There is no clean resolution to this story. Salt Typhoon remains active — as of early 2026, despite sanctions, despite the ten-million-dollar bounty, and despite public attribution by every major Western intelligence agency, the group continues to breach telecommunications networks around the world. The FCC has acknowledged that vulnerabilities are still being exploited. Security researchers have warned that some of the compromises may never be fully remediated, that networks of the scale involved make complete eradication effectively impossible to verify, and that in some cases the attackers may simply have built access paths too deeply embedded to find. CALEA remains law, and the next group willing to use it against America is already looking for a way in.
